where he's the divorce attorney, comes into his office late in the day, close to Christmas. I think it was Christmas Eve if I'm not mistaken, and wants a divorce. And for whatever reasons, probably the time of the year or whatever, rather than taking him as a client, DeVito decides to talk him out of getting a divorce. But he doesn't try and talk him out of getting him a divorce. He tells him the story of the War of the Roses. And then when it's all done, he doesn't have to talk the guy out of getting a divorce. The guy is out the door like a rocket to go back and try doing everything but getting a divorce. Now, you could sit and try and talk somebody out of getting a divorce for days and not get anywhere, but you tell them the right story, you may in fact influence their thinking and their behavior. So the story is the source of all power in selling from the stage in person and certainly in writing. The example I use here is very few people can tell you any of the facts much about Robin Hood, but they have a story in mind about Robin Hood. In our world where everybody's been exposed to thinking and growing. I got a hundred dollar bill in my pocket, can anybody name the 17 principles? How many can you name? But everybody can tell the story. Everybody knows the story behind the book. Nobody knows the content of the book. So you better be imprinting what you want to imprint with story because you have not got a prayer of imprinting it with core content or teaching. That won't stick. In part for me, the reason the three letter things stuck, two stories, the bill collector story and the end signature story where it's wrapped into the front of Al the Plummer. So they remember those stories. They come and ask to hear the Al the Plummer story again, but if I omitted the two stories and just taught the thing, it never would have stuck. How much of that was true versus the Al story? Of what? The Al story? Oh, the only truth in the story. The tears of truth in the story. I got a letter in the mail from Joel Weldon, who's another speaker who lives in Scottsdale, who I did not have a good relationship. I didn't know him. I mean, I didn't have a bad relationship with him. I just kind of casually knew him. And so here comes the letter. I reckon, you know, it's from a peer. So it went in my A-pile. I opened it. What's Joel writing to me about? It was individually addressed. And it was a letter from Joel telling everybody about his plumber in Phoenix. And if you ever need a... So that part was real. The rest of it is complete fiction. I knew I had something. And then I wanted to tie together everything I'd done in the speech in one story. So I made the rest of it up. I never called the plumber. The plumber never came to my house. He didn't do the audit. There's all that's bullshit. He might be doing it. I don't know. But I made it all up. I made it all up. The Bill Collector story is 100% real. So now you're going to ask me if I feel guilty about making up two thirds of the story, aren't you? Okay. Yeah, you know the answer. That's right. So here's a few pages out of the stuck book about the component parts of mental imprint. One of them, by the way, going back to what we were talking about before, is concreteness. No wiggle room if you want stuff to stick. On page 70, there's a little thing out of the power book that is something we miss a lot. And that is the visual aspect of what we do. So symbolism, be it actual pictures or word pictures, when you're doing a lot of face-to-face selling, the multi-level and human potential get rich where I grew up, now all of the physical things are very, very important. I don't bother with any of them anymore. But at the time, the car you drove, the book people found in the pocket of the seat when they rode in your back seat, your wristwatch, your pen, your briefcase, your shoes, all of that was extremely, extremely important. To me now, it's unimportant except when I'm on the floor of a super conference or an info summit. But otherwise, it's unimportant. Now word pictures or the symbolism that comes from word pictures is extremely important. But the point is, is that people are essentially reducing things down to a symbol or an object, a little snapshot that they can contain in their head that represents what you are and what you do. And so you have to give a lot of thought to the word pictures you create, the symbolism that you create, the things that you do in the environments where they are physically interacting with you. So all the way to, like Bill does a spectacular job at our events that hardly anybody copies, by the way, which is always entertaining to me, that they see it but they don't see it, with the visual of the room. And so the signs aren't chosen by accident. The signs that they're going to be stared at for three days are up there for a reason. The signage in the sales area, you know, that's for a reason. The way product is displayed in package is for a reason. And so you have to think through the visual aspects of what you do, not just the words on paper aspects of what you do. They all have to do with imprint. So when you set out to describe something, so like the example I stuck in here on page 71 is from my time book. So when I talk about time vampires, people instantly in their minds have a picture. Now a lot of times, if I have a picture, we show them too of the Dracula looking guy with the big fangs and the big clock, but I don't really have to do that. That's a phrase that people instantly get and it sticks with them. Now they are talking to me about the time vampires they have at home in their office. And of course I describe different times of them, different types of them, and they say it back to me. The biggest problem I got is birth of sucking all the blood out of me every time I come into the office. And so that's a great mental picture made out of words that has real staying power that people can remember when I did media, what little media I do, when I did media for that book. Every interviewer, that's the thing. If they actually did read the book, that's what they pulled out that they wanted to talk about was the time vampire thing. All the articles that we got placed were about the time vampire thing. The other book, page 72 that kind of relates to this, is the Culture Code book. How many of you read the Culture Code? Really, only a few of you. Okay, well there's a little piece of this that's really wacky because this guy is selling to corporate America, so he's created stuff that lying in the room with the pillows and this is all window dressing in my opinion, but there's an underlying lesson there by the way. I mean you can never make what you deliver look too simple and too easy to do because now you've removed all the mysticism. It's why I never write copy in front of anybody. It's not particularly impressive when you actually see somebody do it, especially if they do it fast. They prefer to think you're bleeding and taking days. So in a consulting day, I ain't sitting there writing copy even if I could because it's a bad idea. So he's got some stuff in here that he talks about doing that, you know, but his core stuff is all about cracking the code of the customer, of the person you're trying to influence at a very deep psychological level. So the example I stuck in here is, and the core part of his process is right too, so for example this is talking about the Jeep and doing some work for Chrysler on the Jeep and not asking people what they wanted in a car or in a Jeep, but starting the conversation with what their memories were about Jeeps. So what's already embedded in here about a Jeep, well a lot of people have a lot of stuff embedded about a Jeep, a kid stuff, stuff from TV, etc. And ultimately in his example he boils down that in the United States the person's code, the thing they associate the Jeep with is a horse. And now that colors everything you do to sell Jeeps to people. And the book is well, well worth reading because it is really all about how to get to the mental imprint that's already there and connecting to it with the mental imprint you want to create. I'm surprised more of you haven't read the book. And I would encourage you to do so. Let's get...
